sessions.title: Kickoff
sessions.set_the_stage:
Welcome. Look around this room. Twenty-five people, two days, four weeks, one Demo Day. That is the shape of what you have signed up for.

Most workshops end the moment the room empties. This one starts there. The weekend is the launch pad. The four weeks after are where the deeper builds happen, on your own time, with structured support. Demo Day is where you stand in front of the room and show what you shipped.

Thirty minutes to set expectations, then we start building.

questions.title: What do you want to walk out with on Demo Day?
questions.body: Not a vague aspiration. A specific sentence. By Demo Day I will have a portfolio that demonstrates I can _______. Write it down. Keep it visible all six weeks.

questions.title: What is the one thing that could stop you?
questions.body: Be honest. Time? Confidence? A specific tool you have never used? Knowing the obstacle now lets you ask for help before it blocks you.

build.title: The program shape
build.instructions: Two days in this room. Then Week 1 chatbot, Weeks 2-3 agent, Week 4 Demo Day prep. Office hours every week, 90 minutes, in this room or on Meet. WhatsApp group for between-session unblocks. Demo Day in Week 5.

build.title: The portfolio at the end
build.instructions: Six builds, one URL. AI app, website, chatbot, audio piece, founder video, automation agent. All live. All yours. The URL is what you share with recruiters and on LinkedIn.

build.title: How the room runs
build.instructions: When you are stuck for more than fifteen minutes, raise your hand. Do not power through silently — the facilitator walks the room precisely to catch the silent stuck. The fastest way to slow yourself down is to refuse help.

sessions.title: Find & validate the idea
sessions.set_the_stage:
Ninety minutes to land on the one idea you will build for the next six weeks. Some of you walked in with an idea. Some of you did not. Both are fine. By 11:00 every person in this room has a single sentence on a card: the problem, who has it, why their current workaround is not good enough.

The biggest myth about entrepreneurship is that you need a flash of genius. You do not. You need a problem you actually care about, and a small honest description of who has it. Today, AI is the easy part. The hard part is choosing what to point it at.

Thirty minutes to surface candidates with Gemini. Thirty minutes to test which one survives a real human. Thirty minutes to commit on a card.

questions.title: Whose problems do you already understand?
questions.body: Not who you want to help. Who do you already know. Write down three groups of people whose daily lives you can describe in some detail. Could be students at your college, freelance designers, parents of toddlers, people who run small kirana stores, weekend cyclists, anyone.

questions.title: What annoys you, personally?
questions.body: List five things that have made you genuinely irritated in the last month. Tiny annoyances count. Forgetting where you parked. Spending an hour comparing three insurance policies. Not being able to find someone to play badminton with on a Sunday morning.

questions.title: What do you keep wishing existed?
questions.body: Sometimes the cleanest signal is the phrase someone wishes existed. Write down two or three things you have thought I wish there was a thing that did this. Do not filter for whether it could exist or already does. Just write what came to mind.

techniques: Picture the specific person with the problem. Where are they? What time of day is it? What just happened?
What are they feeling in that moment? Frustration, embarrassment, exhaustion, resignation?
Could the room recognise this person if they walked in?
Is AI genuinely useful here, or are you forcing it?
Watch for the AI-for-the-sake-of-AI idea, where you find a problem to justify the tech.
Watch for the spreadsheet problem, where a form would solve it just as well.
Does someone currently pay money to have this problem managed?
Does someone currently spend significant time on it?
Does someone currently experience a painful consequence when it is not solved?

prompts.title: Find your idea
prompts.content:
I am about to spend six weeks building a real AI-powered product as a solopreneur. I do not have an idea yet. Help me find one.

Here is what I know about myself.

Three groups of people whose lives I understand: [from Question 1]
Five things that have annoyed me in the last month: [from Question 2]
Two or three things I wish existed: [from Question 3]

Do four things for me.

First, look at the annoyances and the wishes. Tell me which two or three of them point to a real problem that other people probably also experience. Not just me. Be honest if any of them are too personal or too niche to be a real micro-venture.

Second, for each of those candidate problems, describe in two sentences who specifically has this problem. The more specific the better. Not 'people' or 'professionals'. A specific kind of person in a specific situation.

Third, for each candidate, name an existing tool or workaround people currently use, and one thing about that workaround that is genuinely not good enough.

Fourth, recommend the one problem out of these candidates that you think is most worth me exploring this weekend, and tell me honestly why. Then ask me three follow-up questions to help me get sharper on that problem before I move on.

build.title: Read Gemini's response slowly
build.instructions: Pay attention to the candidate it recommends, and also pay attention to which one made you sit up a little when you read it. Sometimes Gemini's pick is right. Sometimes your gut is right. Trust your gut if it disagrees, but only if you can explain why.

build.title: Have a second round
build.instructions: Answer Gemini's three follow-up questions. Push back when its picks feel off. One or two more turns of back-and-forth is usually enough.

build.title: Talk to a real human
build.instructions: Find one person in the room you do not know. Tell them your one-sentence idea. Watch their face, not their words. If their first reaction is 'oh, yeah, that would be useful for X' you are close. If they nod politely and ask a generic question, you are not there yet.

build.title: Write your card
build.instructions: By 11:00 you have three things on a card. One sentence about the problem. One sentence about who specifically has it. One sentence about why their current workaround is not good enough.

test_output.title: The problem test
test_output.description: Can you say the problem in one sentence without mentioning your solution? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The person test
test_output.description: Can you describe one specific kind of person who has this problem, in enough detail that a stranger in the room could recognise them? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The gap test
test_output.description: Can you name what people currently do about this problem, and explain why it is not good enough? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The energy test
test_output.description: Does this idea make you a little excited to spend six weeks on it? If yes, pass. If no, run the prompt again with a different framing.

sessions.title: Build the AI app — get it working
sessions.set_the_stage:
You have a problem. You have a user. Now you build.

Today, a solopreneur with a clear idea and the right prompts can have a working AI app in two hours. Not a mockup. Not a wireframe. Something real that runs in a browser, takes a real input, and gives back a real output.

One HTML file. No code knowledge required. No backend. You build it with Claude, customise it until it feels like yours. By 13:15 the app responds to your test input with something recognisably useful — even if it is rough. We polish next session. Right now: get to working.

Lunch is at 13:15. Aim to be hungry by then, not panicking.

questions.title: What is the simplest version of your app?
questions.body: Before you write a prompt, sketch the smallest thing that could be useful. One input field. One output. One AI call in between. If you cannot describe it in one sentence, it is too complicated.

questions.title: What does the user put in?
questions.body: Be specific. A paragraph of text? A photo of their thali? A list of five preferences? The shape of the input determines the shape of everything else.

questions.title: What does the user get out?
questions.body: Be specific. A list of seven items? A 200-word plan? A flashcard set? A score from 1 to 10 with one line of advice? Write the example output by hand, before the AI ever runs.

techniques: Open the app as if you have never seen it. Is it immediately obvious what to do?
Read every label and button out loud. Does it sound like something a real person would say?
Would a real user complete the full journey without help?
Run the app with a real input, not a perfect test input. What happens?
Run the app with an input it was not designed for. Does it fail gracefully?
Run the same input twice. Do you get the same quality of output?
Look at the output for three seconds and look away. What did you retain?
If someone paid for this output, would they feel it was worth it?
Would your target user screenshot the output and send it to a colleague?

prompts.title: Build your AI app — first version
prompts.content:
I am building a single-page AI app as a solopreneur. I need a single HTML file I can deploy by dragging into Netlify. No backend, no API key — the AI call happens through the OpenRouter free tier with a public key you will give me.

Here is the idea.

The user: [one sentence — the specific person]
The problem: [one sentence — what is broken for them today]
The input the user gives me: [one specific input, with an example]
The output I give back: [one specific output, with an example]

Write me a single index.html file that does this. Inside the file, include:

A clean simple form for the input.
A single button labelled with something specific, not 'Submit'.
A loading state while the AI thinks.
The output rendered clearly below the form.
A system prompt for the AI that is detailed enough to produce consistently good output for the specific user and specific problem above.
Styling that is clean and readable on a phone. Not flashy. Just good.

At the top of the file, leave a comment block with the system prompt, so I can edit it later without digging through the code.

build.title: Get the file
build.instructions: Copy Claude's HTML into a new file called index.html. Open it in your browser by double-clicking. It should load. If it does not load, paste the error back to Claude and ask for a fix.

build.title: Run it with a real input
build.instructions: Not a clever test input. Not the example from your prompt. A real one — the actual thing your target user would type in. See what comes back.

build.title: Sharpen the system prompt
build.instructions: If the output is generic, the system prompt is generic. Tell Claude what was wrong with the output and ask it to rewrite the system prompt to be more specific. Test again. Iterate until the output is recognisably useful, even if it is still rough.

test_output.title: The first-run test
test_output.description: Did your app produce any output for a real input by 13:00? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The relevance test
test_output.description: Is the output recognisably about the problem, for the user you described? Not perfect — recognisable. If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The honesty test
test_output.description: Would you show this to your target user without apologising for it? Not yet. That is fine. Pass means you know what to fix next.

sessions.title: Milestone check — your AI does the right thing for the right person
sessions.set_the_stage:
Thirty minutes. Everyone in the room shows two things.

One. The user you described this morning. One sentence. Read it aloud.
Two. The most recent output your AI produced for a realistic input from that user. Read it aloud.

The room and the facilitator listen for one thing: does the output sound like it was produced for that specific person, doing that specific thing? Or does it sound like a generic AI response with the topic swapped in?

The biggest failure mode on Day 1 is students who get the AI wired up and stop there, then spend the afternoon on UI polish. This thirty-minute interrupt is designed to catch that. If your output is generic, the system prompt is the problem, not the styling.

Facilitator walks the room. Stuck students get unstuck. By 14:15 every person knows whether they are on track or whether they need to course-correct the prompt before they go further.

build.title: Read aloud
build.instructions: When the facilitator gets to you, read the user sentence and the most recent output aloud to the room. The room listens. The facilitator names one thing to keep, one thing to fix.

build.title: Course correct or keep building
build.instructions: If your output is on track, you keep building in the next session. If your output is generic, your next ninety minutes go to sharpening the system prompt before any visual polish. The facilitator will tell you which.

test_output.title: The recognition test
test_output.description: Could a stranger in the room hear your output and recognise it as being for the user you described? If yes, pass. If no, the system prompt needs work before you move on.

sessions.title: Build the AI app — ship it
sessions.set_the_stage:
By the end of this block your AI app is live on the internet, at a real URL, working on a phone.

Two things happen in the next hour and forty-five minutes. First, you tighten the output so it consistently does the right thing — not just once, but every time, for the full range of inputs your user might throw at it. Second, you deploy it. Netlify drag-and-drop. Three minutes. You walk out of this session with a URL you can share.

Do not get pulled into visual perfection. The product works or it does not. Once it works, deploy. The website session at 16:15 is where the visual story gets told, not here.

questions.title: What does the AI never do?
questions.body: A good system prompt has constraints. What is off-limits? What should the AI refuse, redirect, or politely decline? Without constraints, the AI drifts. With them, it stays useful.

questions.title: Where does it currently fail?
questions.body: Throw three weird inputs at your app. Inputs your user might realistically give that you did not design for. Where does it break? Where does it produce something embarrassing?

prompts.title: Tighten the system prompt
prompts.content:
Here is my current index.html. The system prompt is in the comment block at the top.

[paste your full index.html]

The output is good when the user gives a clean input. The output gets weak when the user gives [describe the kind of input that fails — short, vague, off-topic, etc.].

Rewrite the system prompt so the app produces good output in both cases. Add explicit constraints for what the AI should refuse to do, redirect, or handle gracefully. Keep the output format consistent.

Give me only the updated system prompt, not the whole file. I will paste it in myself.

build.title: Update the system prompt
build.instructions: Replace the system prompt in your index.html with Claude's rewrite. Run the three weird inputs again. Should be visibly better.

build.title: Polish the labels and one moment
build.instructions: Read every label, button, and instruction on the page out loud. Replace any AI-flavoured phrasing with something a real person would say. Pick the one moment of the user's journey that matters most — the moment they see the output — and make sure that moment feels considered. Not everything. Just that one moment.

build.title: Deploy to Netlify
build.instructions: Go to app.netlify.com/drop. Drag your index.html into the box. Wait fifteen seconds. Copy the URL. Open it on your phone, ideally on mobile data so you know it works outside wifi.

build.title: Send the URL to one person
build.instructions: Pick one person who is not in this room. Send them the URL on WhatsApp with one line: 'I built this in a day. Tell me what you think.' Their reply might land before Demo Day. If it does, it is gold for your pitch.

test_output.title: The URL test
test_output.description: Do you have a netlify.app URL that opens on your phone? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The strangers test
test_output.description: Could a stranger open the URL and complete the full journey without you sitting next to them? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The output test
test_output.description: For three different realistic inputs, did your app produce three outputs that were all useful? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The phone test
test_output.description: Does the app look good and work cleanly on a phone, not just a laptop? If yes, pass.

output_capture.ask: Paste the live URL of your AI App.
output_capture.guidance: This is the URL you got from Netlify after dragging your index.html into it. It should open on any phone without needing a login.

sessions.title: Plan the website + marketing assets
sessions.set_the_stage:
You ship the website tomorrow morning. Tonight, you plan it.

A solopreneur without a website is a shopkeeper without a shopfront. Your website is a conversation with a stranger who has never heard of you. A conversation that has to answer three questions in thirty seconds. What is this? Is this for me? What do I do next?

The biggest reason websites take twice as long as they should is that founders try to write copy live, in the website builder, while staring at a blank page. We are not doing that. In the next hour and forty-five minutes you write the entire website copy in a doc. You write the video script. You sketch the brochure. By 18:00 you walk out with three documents, ready to drop into the tools tomorrow morning.

No Carrd tonight. No recording. No design. Just writing.

questions.title: What does your website say in 30 seconds?
questions.body: If a stranger gives you thirty seconds, what three things must they take away? Write the three sentences. These become your headline, sub-headline, and call to action.

questions.title: What is the one specific outcome you promise?
questions.body: Not 'helps you cook better.' A specific, namable outcome. 'A seven-day meal plan built around what is in your kitchen.' 'Three tailored resume versions in five minutes.' Specific outcomes pull people in. Vague benefits push them away.

questions.title: What does your video say in 90 seconds?
questions.body: Three beats. The problem you saw. The thing you built. What it does for someone. Ninety seconds, three beats, thirty seconds each. Write the script as if you were speaking to one person.

techniques: Count 'you' versus 'I' or 'we'. 'You' should appear far more than 'I' or 'we'.
Would your target user read the headline and think 'this is written about me'?
Read the copy out loud as if speaking directly to your target user.
Does the website describe what the product actually does, not what you wish it did?
Does the how-it-works section describe what the AI does in plain language?
After reading, does the visitor have an accurate expectation of what will happen?
After thirty seconds, can someone say what the product does and who it is for?
Does the website name a specific outcome?
Does the website help the visitor understand why this is better than their current workaround?

prompts.title: Draft the website copy
prompts.content:
I am writing the copy for the single-page website of an AI product I just built. I want the copy first, in a doc. Tomorrow morning I will drop it into Carrd.

The product: [one sentence — what it does]
The user: [one sentence — who specifically uses it]
The outcome they get: [one sentence — the specific thing they walk away with]
The live URL of the AI app: [paste your Netlify URL]

Write me the copy for a single-page Carrd site with these sections:

Headline — under ten words, names the outcome, sounds like a real person said it.
Sub-headline — one sentence, names the specific user.
How it works — three short steps, plain language, what the user does and what they get back.
Why this is better than what they do today — three sentences naming the current workaround and why it is not good enough.
Call to action — one button, specific verb, leads to the AI app URL.

Avoid the AI-website voice. No 'revolutionary', no 'powerful', no 'seamless'. Write it like one person describing what they made, to one other person.

prompts.title: Draft the 90-second video script
prompts.content:
I am recording a 90-second founder video tomorrow morning. Screen recording of me using the product, with me narrating over it. I will use the script you write tonight to record cleanly tomorrow.

The product: [paste from previous prompt]
The user: [paste]
The outcome: [paste]

Write me a 90-second script in three beats of 30 seconds each.

Beat 1 (seconds 0-30) — The problem I saw. Be specific. A real situation, not abstract pain. End on the sentence that names what I built.

Beat 2 (seconds 30-60) — What it does. I am showing my screen during this beat. Tell me what I am doing on screen and what I am saying out loud at each moment.

Beat 3 (seconds 60-90) — Who this is for, and what they get. End with my AI app URL spoken aloud.

Write it in spoken English, not written English. Short sentences. Real pauses. No 'in conclusion' or 'in summary'. The way a person actually talks.

Flag any line that will be hard to say in one take and suggest a smoother alternative.

prompts.title: Outline a one-page brochure
prompts.content:
I am generating a one-page brochure tomorrow morning. PDF. I will use Canva or a similar tool. I need the text content tonight so tomorrow I just drop it into a template.

The product: [paste]
The user: [paste]
The outcome: [paste]
The URL: [paste]

Give me the text for a one-page brochure, in the order it should appear, with each block labelled:

Hero line — same as website headline, can be slightly different if it works better in print.
The problem — three sentences.
The product — three sentences.
How it works — three bullets.
Who this is for — one sentence.
Call to action — one line with the URL.

Keep the total word count under 250 words. A brochure is read at arm's length, not studied.

build.title: Save three documents
build.instructions: Three Google Docs or Notion pages. Website copy. Video script. Brochure copy. Title them clearly. You will need them in your hand tomorrow at 9:00am.

build.title: Read everything out loud, once
build.instructions: Read the website copy out loud. Read the video script out loud. Read the brochure copy out loud. Whatever sounds awkward gets fixed tonight, not tomorrow.

build.title: Show one peer in the room
build.instructions: Find one person, swap your video scripts. Read theirs. Tell them the one sentence that does not yet sound like a real human saying it. Receive the same. Edit.

test_output.title: The three-docs test
test_output.description: Do you have three documents saved with website copy, video script, brochure copy? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The thirty-second test
test_output.description: If a stranger read only your headline and sub-headline, would they know what your product does and who it is for? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The spoken test
test_output.description: Does your video script sound like spoken English, not written English? Read it once more if you are unsure. If yes, pass.

sessions.title: Ship the website, audio, video, and brochure
sessions.set_the_stage:
Six hours. You walked in with three documents. You walk out at 15:00 with a live website, an audio piece embedded on it, a founder video on LinkedIn, and a one-page brochure as a PDF.

This is the execution day. Everything has been planned. Today you assemble.

The facilitator walks the room continuously. The order matters: website first because everything else gets linked to it, then audio because it embeds on the site, then video because it lives independently on LinkedIn and gets linked from the site, then brochure because it can be done in parallel during any wait.

Lunch happens inside this block. Take it when your flow allows, not on a forced schedule. Aim for around 12:30, not later than 13:00.

We come back together at 15:00 for the interim demo. Hard deadline. The interim demo is the most important hour of the weekend — it is what makes the next four weeks have momentum. Be done by 14:55.

questions.title: What is the one section of your website that has to sing?
questions.body: The headline and the call to action. Everything else can be average. Those two have to be precise.

techniques: Open your published Carrd site as if you have never seen it. Where do your eyes go first?
Read every word aloud. Cut anything that sounds like marketing.
Does the founder video sound like the person who wrote the website?
Test the link from the website to the AI app. Does it open in a new tab and load?
Test the audio. Does it play on a phone, not just a laptop?
Test the LinkedIn post. Does the video autoplay in the feed?
Does the whole presence — site, audio, video, brochure — feel like it was made by the same person?
Would a recruiter who landed on this take it seriously?
Does every asset point the visitor toward trying the AI app?

prompts.title: Website production helper
prompts.content:
I have my website copy in a doc. I am about to drop it into Carrd. Walk me through, in order, what to do — create the account, pick a template, paste each section in the right place, link the AI app button to my Netlify URL, embed an audio file, embed a video, publish.

Keep it tight. Tell me the click sequence, not the theory.

prompts.title: Audio production
prompts.content:
I want to generate a 60-second audio piece from this script.

[paste your video script — the first 60 seconds, or a tightened version]

Give me the ElevenLabs settings to use — voice selection, stability, similarity, style — to make it sound like a calm, real person and not a typical AI voice-over. I want it warm, slightly understated, and one notch slower than the default pace.

build.title: Hour 1 — Website skeleton
build.instructions: Carrd account. Template chosen. All your copy pasted into the right sections. Link to the AI app live. Site published to a yourname.carrd.co URL. Do not polish yet — just get it up.

build.title: Hour 2 — Audio recorded and embedded
build.instructions: 60 seconds of audio generated in ElevenLabs, downloaded as MP3, embedded on the Carrd site below the headline. Test on a phone.

build.title: Hour 3 — Founder video recorded and posted
build.instructions: Loom or QuickTime screen recording, you narrating live with the script in your peripheral vision. Aim for 90 seconds. Re-record up to three times if needed, but no more. Upload to LinkedIn as a native video post with a one-line caption. Pin the post to your profile. Copy the post URL — you will paste it on the site.

build.title: Hour 4 — Brochure to PDF
build.instructions: Canva, one-page template. Drop in the brochure copy. Export PDF. Upload the PDF to Drive, set link to anyone-with-link can view. Copy the link, add a 'Download brochure' button to the website pointing to the PDF.

build.title: Hour 5 — Polish the website
build.instructions: Now you polish. Whitespace. Fonts. Mobile preview. One photo of you in the header if you want. Test every link. Test on a friend's phone.

build.title: Final 30 minutes — Smoke test
build.instructions: Open your website on a phone, on mobile data. Click the AI app button. Use the app. Come back. Click the LinkedIn video. Watch it. Download the brochure. Listen to the audio. The full visitor journey, in one walkthrough. Anything that does not work gets fixed before 14:55.

test_output.title: The website test
test_output.description: Does yourname.carrd.co load on a phone, with headline, copy, audio, video link, brochure link, and a button to the AI app? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The audio test
test_output.description: Does the audio play when you tap the play button on a phone? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The video test
test_output.description: Is the founder video live on your LinkedIn, with the post pinned, and linked from your website? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The brochure test
test_output.description: Does the brochure download as a PDF from the website link? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The full-journey test
test_output.description: Did you walk a friend through the full journey — site, audio, video, brochure, AI app — on a phone, before 15:00? If yes, pass.

output_capture.ask: Paste the live URL of your Carrd website.
output_capture.guidance: This is the published Carrd URL, not the editor URL. Confirm it works on a phone and that the audio, video link, brochure link, and AI app button all work.

sessions.title: Interim Demo Day
sessions.set_the_stage:
Stop building. Stop polishing. Stand up.

For the last day and a half you have been heads-down. Now the room shifts. Every person in this cohort has 90 seconds to show what they made.

This is not Demo Day. Demo Day is in four weeks, with the full portfolio, recorded, shareable, in front of guests. This is the interim demo — peer to peer, no slides, no rehearsal. You stand up, open your site on the screen, walk the room through the user's journey in 90 seconds, and sit down. The room claps. The next person stands up.

This hour is the most important hour of the weekend. Not because of the demos themselves, but because of what watching twenty-five demos does to your own bar. By the time the room finishes, you will have ideas for what to add over the next four weeks. Write them down as you watch.

build.title: The 90-second demo format
build.instructions: Open your Carrd site on the projector. Three beats. Beat 1: who this is for and what they were doing before. Beat 2: open the AI app and run one real input — the room watches the output appear. Beat 3: one thing you learned building this that surprised you. Sit down. The room claps.

build.title: Watch and write
build.instructions: While others demo, keep a doc open. Whenever you see an idea you want to steal — copy a section structure, a phrasing, a moment in the AI output — write it down. You will look at this list during the four-week stretch.

build.title: Three demos get the room's vote
build.instructions: At the end of the hour, the room votes for three demos that stood out — one for clearest problem, one for tightest AI output, one for sharpest delivery. Not winners. Just signal.

test_output.title: The demo test
test_output.description: Did every person in the cohort demo in under 90 seconds? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The notebook test
test_output.description: Did you write down at least five ideas from other people's demos to apply to your own build over the next four weeks? If yes, pass.

sessions.title: The four-week plan
sessions.set_the_stage:
You walked in two days ago with an idea on a card. You leave with a live AI app, a website, an audio piece, a founder video, and a brochure. Five real things. All live. All yours.

The next four weeks are where the harder builds happen. Chatbot. Agent. Then Demo Day. You will do most of this on your own, with two forms of support: a weekly 90-minute office hours session — this room, same time, every week — and a WhatsApp group for between-session unblocks.

Forty-five minutes to hand over the materials, walk through the four-week shape, set the norms for how we support you, and lock the Demo Day date on every calendar.

questions.title: What hour of the week is yours?
questions.body: Pick a specific hour, every week, that is your Just Build hour. Not 'whenever I find time'. A specific hour. Put it in your calendar before you leave the room. The students who finish the four weeks are the ones who treated this hour as non-negotiable.

questions.title: Who is your accountability partner?
questions.body: Pair up with one person in the room. Once a week, on your Just Build hour, you text each other one update — what you finished, what you are stuck on. Two-sentence accountability. Pick now. Exchange numbers.

build.title: Hand-out: the chatbot PDF
build.instructions: Every student gets the chatbot build PDF on their email and on WhatsApp. Detailed, step-by-step, screenshots for every screen. The expectation is you complete the chatbot build in Week 1 and bring questions to the Week 1 office hours.

build.title: Hand-out: the agent PDF
build.instructions: Every student gets the agent build PDF on their email and on WhatsApp. This is the harder build. Two weeks. Bring questions to the Week 2 and Week 3 office hours.

build.title: Office hours: same room, every week
build.instructions: This room, this time, every Saturday, for the next four weeks. 90 minutes. Facilitator walks through common stuck points proactively. The rest is live Q&A. Treat it as a meeting, not a class — you bring your screen, your question, your unblock.

build.title: WhatsApp group: norms
build.instructions: Group created tonight. Post screenshots, not text descriptions. 'It is not working' with no screenshot gets ignored. Screenshot plus what you tried plus the error message gets a reply within a few hours. Office hours are for stuck-for-30-minutes problems. WhatsApp is for quick unblocks.

build.title: Demo Day date and shape
build.instructions: Demo Day is locked: [DATE]. This room. 6pm. Guests invited — alumni, recruiters, family if you want. Each of you gets 3 minutes plus 1 minute Q&A, hard cutoff. Demos are recorded. The recording becomes part of your portfolio.

test_output.title: The calendar test
test_output.description: Is your weekly Just Build hour in your calendar? Are the four office hours dates in your calendar? Is Demo Day in your calendar? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The partner test
test_output.description: Do you know one person's name and number in this room, who you have agreed to check in with weekly? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The materials test
test_output.description: Do you have the chatbot PDF, the agent PDF, and the WhatsApp invite link on your phone before you leave this room? If yes, pass.

sessions.title: Office hours — Chatbot
sessions.set_the_stage:
Welcome back. Week 1.

By now everyone in the room has worked through the chatbot PDF. Some of you have a chatbot live on your Carrd site. Some of you are stuck. Both are normal at this point.

Ninety minutes. The first thirty minutes I walk through the four places students most commonly get stuck on the chatbot build. The next forty-five is live Q&A — screen up, question on the table. The last fifteen we close with a roster check — every person in the room confirms, out loud, whether their chatbot is live or what specifically they need to land it by next Saturday.
agenda: First 30 min — Walkthrough of the four common stuck points on the chatbot build
Next 45 min — Live Q&A. Screens up. Bring your specific question
Last 15 min — Roster check. Every person says: live, or specifically what is left

stuck_points.title: The system prompt is too short
stuck_points.resolution: A chatbot system prompt of three sentences produces a chatbot that says useful things three times before going generic. The PDF has the long-form template. Use it. If yours is short, expand it before doing anything else.

stuck_points.title: The chatbot has no scope
stuck_points.resolution: Without an explicit 'do not answer questions outside X' instruction, the chatbot answers anything and feels like generic ChatGPT. Add the scope sentence at the top of the system prompt.

stuck_points.title: The embed code does not show up on Carrd
stuck_points.resolution: Carrd's free tier blocks some script tags. Use the iframe embed option instead, not the script embed. The PDF has the exact iframe HTML.

stuck_points.title: The chatbot never points to the AI app
stuck_points.resolution: The chatbot exists to convert visitors into AI app users. Every long answer should end with a soft invitation back to the app. Add that instruction to the system prompt explicitly.

test_output.title: The live test
test_output.description: By next Saturday, is your chatbot embedded on your Carrd site, visible on a phone, and producing useful answers for three test questions? If yes, pass. If no, post a screenshot to WhatsApp before Wednesday.

sessions.live_qa_note: Open the floor for 40-45 minutes. Screens up. Specific questions only. If someone has a general 'I am stuck' question, ask them to share their screen and walk through what they tried.
sessions.homework_check: Every person, around the room, in one sentence: chatbot live by next Saturday, or specifically what is left.

output_capture.ask: Paste your website URL again, now with the chatbot embedded.
output_capture.guidance: The chatbot lives on your Carrd site as an embedded widget. Confirm it appears when you open the URL on your phone.

sessions.title: Office hours — Agent, week 1
sessions.set_the_stage:
Week 2. The agent build is harder than the chatbot build. That is by design. You have two weeks to ship it, not one.

This week you should be roughly halfway through the agent PDF — your Make.com account is set up, the template is cloned, the trigger from your Carrd form is wired, and you have run one test scenario end to end, even if the email it sends is still ugly.

The four stuck points in the first half of the agent build are different from the second half. We walk through this week's four. Next week we walk through the back-half four.
agenda: First 30 min — Walkthrough of the four common stuck points in the first half of the agent build
Next 45 min — Live Q&A. Screens up. Make.com scenarios open
Last 15 min — Roster check. Every person says: trigger working, or specifically what is left

stuck_points.title: The Carrd form is not triggering the Make scenario
stuck_points.resolution: Make.com's webhook needs the exact URL pasted into Carrd's form-action field, not the form-redirect field. The PDF page 8 shows the exact place. Check there first.

stuck_points.title: The scenario runs but does not see the form fields
stuck_points.resolution: The webhook needs to be re-listened after you change the form. In Make, click 'Redetermine data structure' on the webhook module, then submit the form once. The fields will appear.

stuck_points.title: The free tier is hitting the operations limit
stuck_points.resolution: Free tier is 1,000 ops per month — more than enough. If you are hitting limits, you are probably running the same scenario in a loop. Check whether you accidentally connected the scenario's output back to its trigger.

stuck_points.title: The email module asks for a Gmail connection and the connection fails
stuck_points.resolution: Gmail OAuth requires the Google account to allow third-party access. The PDF has the settings link. Open it in the same browser where you are logged into Make.

test_output.title: The end-to-end test
test_output.description: By next Saturday, can you submit your Carrd form and have an email arrive in your inbox within 30 seconds? Content can be rough. The pipe must be live. If yes, pass.

sessions.live_qa_note: Open the floor for 40-45 minutes. Screens up — Make.com scenarios visible. Most agent debugging happens by looking at the scenario diagram together.
sessions.homework_check: Every person, around the room, in one sentence: trigger-to-email working by next Saturday, or specifically what is left.

sessions.title: Office hours — Agent, week 2
sessions.set_the_stage:
Week 3. The agent should be live by next Saturday.

The back half of the agent build is where the agent goes from 'sending an email' to 'sending an email that feels written for this specific person'. The plumbing is done. The voice is what is left.

This week's stuck points are about quality, not connectivity. The email module works. The question is whether what it sends sounds like the same person who wrote the website copy and narrated the video — or like a no-reply notification from an internet company.
agenda: First 30 min — Walkthrough of the four back-half stuck points on the agent build
Next 45 min — Live Q&A. Bring your sample emails. The room critiques
Last 15 min — Roster check. Every person says: agent live, or specifically what is left for Demo Day

stuck_points.title: The email reads like an auto-confirmation
stuck_points.resolution: Pull your website copy. Pull your video script. The agent's voice must match. Rewrite the email body so it sounds like a continuation of the same person, not a transactional ping. Lead with one specific reference to what the user just submitted.

stuck_points.title: The agent does not handle empty or weird inputs
stuck_points.resolution: Add a router module in Make. If the form's main field is empty or under N characters, route to a polite 'I need a bit more to work with' response. The PDF page 22 has the router config.

stuck_points.title: The agent fires twice for one submission
stuck_points.resolution: Carrd's default form behaviour submits and refreshes. If you have 'submit and stay' enabled, some browsers fire twice. Switch to redirect mode and point to a thank-you page in Carrd.

stuck_points.title: Three test runs is not enough
stuck_points.resolution: Run the full scenario ten times before Demo Day, with different realistic inputs. Run it with your name. Run it with a friend's name. Run it from a phone. Each run reveals something the previous nine did not. If anything fails, fix it now — not the morning of Demo Day.

test_output.title: The live agent test
test_output.description: By Demo Day, can you submit your Carrd form three times with different inputs and have three good emails arrive — each one sounding like a real person wrote it for that specific submission? If yes, pass.

sessions.live_qa_note: Less debugging this week, more critique. Have students paste sample agent emails into the projector. The room reads them, names what sounds off, suggests fixes.
sessions.homework_check: Every person, around the room: agent fully live for Demo Day, or specifically what is left. Anyone with more than one specific issue gets a 1:1 slot with the facilitator before Demo Day.

output_capture.ask: Save your Carrd form URL (where the agent listens).
output_capture.guidance: The agent lives inside Make.com but listens to your Carrd form. Your portfolio page will show this URL alongside a description of what the agent does. Your facilitator also captures a screenshot of your Make.com scenario for the portfolio.

sessions.title: Office hours — Demo Day prep
sessions.set_the_stage:
Last office hours. Demo Day is one week away.

By now every person in the room has the full portfolio live — AI app, website, audio, video, brochure, chatbot, agent. Six builds, one URL. The work this week is not more building. It is the pitch.

Most demos fail not because the product is weak but because the founder rushed, said too much, and never paused. Today we fix that. Three minutes plus one minute of Q&A — the YC cap. Every person in this room pitches twice, the room gives feedback, and you leave with a tight, rehearsed three-minute demo.
agenda: First 20 min — Walkthrough of the four-beat demo structure and the four most common pitch failures
Next 60 min — Each person pitches twice. Three minutes. Hard cutoff. Room gives one keep, one fix
Last 10 min — Logistics for Demo Day. Recording setup, slide handover, guest list confirmation

stuck_points.title: Saying too much in three minutes
stuck_points.resolution: Three minutes is short. Pick three things. The problem, the product in action (live, on screen, with a real input), and the one validation insight you got from a real user. Everything else gets cut. Anything you cut, the audience can ask about in Q&A.

stuck_points.title: No live demo of the AI app
stuck_points.resolution: The single most powerful 30 seconds of any demo is the moment your AI app produces output, live, on the projector, for an input typed during the pitch. Slides cannot match this. Plan the pitch around it.

stuck_points.title: Pace too fast
stuck_points.resolution: Founders pitching for the first time speak 30 percent faster than they think they are. Practise once with a stopwatch. If you finish in under 2:30, slow down. Pauses are not dead air — they are the moments the audience absorbs.

stuck_points.title: No call to action
stuck_points.resolution: End every demo with one specific ask. 'Open my URL on your phone right now and try it.' 'If you know one parent of a toddler, send them this.' Demos that end on a flat 'thank you' leave no momentum. Demos that end on a specific ask travel.

test_output.title: The three-minute test
test_output.description: Did you deliver your demo twice today, in under three minutes, with a live AI app run inside the pitch? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The feedback test
test_output.description: Did you write down at least three specific things to change before next Saturday's Demo Day? If yes, pass.

sessions.live_qa_note: Hard timekeeping. Stopwatch on. At 2:55 you give the signal. At 3:00 the pitch stops, even mid-sentence. The room internalises the cutoff by watching others get cut.
sessions.homework_check: Every person leaves with: a recorded video of their final pitch attempt (phone is fine), three specific edits to make, and Demo Day confirmed in their calendar.

sessions.title: Demo Day
sessions.set_the_stage:
Look at this room. It looks different from the morning of Day 1. That is intentional.

Five weeks ago you walked in with an idea on a card. You leave tonight with six live builds, collected into one URL, and a three-minute recording of yourself standing in this room demonstrating what you made to a room of strangers who had no reason to be impressed.

That recording becomes the most valuable thing in your portfolio. Recruiters do not read resumes. They watch ninety seconds of footage and decide.

Twenty-five demos. Three minutes plus one minute of Q&A. Hard cap. We record every one. By 8:00pm tonight every one of you has a Demo Day recording on your portfolio URL.

questions.title: What is your one specific ask?
questions.body: Every demo ends with one specific ask of the room. Not 'thank you'. Something the room can do tonight. Try the URL. Forward it to one person. Connect on LinkedIn. Write it down before you pitch.

build.title: The four-beat demo
build.instructions: Beat 1: The problem and the person. 30 seconds. Beat 2: The product, live on screen, with a real input. 90 seconds. Beat 3: The one thing a real user said that you did not expect. 30 seconds. Beat 4: The specific ask. 30 seconds.

build.title: The Q&A minute
build.instructions: One minute for questions from the room. If no one asks, the facilitator asks. If you do not know the answer, say so. 'I do not know yet, but here is how I would find out' is a stronger answer than a guess.

build.title: Record everything
build.instructions: Camera on a tripod at the back of the room. Every demo recorded. Files uploaded the same night. Demo Day recording lives at the top of every student's portfolio URL by midnight.

test_output.title: The cap test
test_output.description: Did every demo come in under 3:00? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The recording test
test_output.description: Is every demo recorded, with audio that can be heard, and the screen capture clearly showing the AI app run? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The ask test
test_output.description: Did every demo end with one specific ask? If yes, pass.

output_capture.ask: Paste the link to the published Demo Day recording on your portfolio.
output_capture.guidance: The recording lives at the top of your portfolio URL. It plays automatically when someone opens the page. The recording is the strongest thing in the portfolio — recruiters watch the demo, then explore the rest.

sessions.title: Close — what comes next
sessions.set_the_stage:
Stop for a moment. Look at the URL on your phone. Six weeks ago this did not exist. Not the product, not the website, not the chatbot, not the audio, not the video, not the agent, not the Demo Day recording.

You came in with an idea on a card. You leave with seven live things, collected into one URL.

This last block is about what you do with that URL across the next twelve months. Internships. Off-campus applications. Placement interviews when they come. LinkedIn that actually pulls recruiters to you. Your portfolio is the proof you can already do AI work. Use it everywhere.

At the very end, for those who want it, we open one more door. Orange Caterpillar Ventures for builders who want to keep building. Selective.

questions.title: What is the one thing you will do tonight with this URL?
questions.body: Before you sleep. One specific action. Post on LinkedIn. Update your resume. Message one senior. Pin it to your WhatsApp status. The portfolio is only as valuable as the number of people who see it.

questions.title: What is the one thing you will do this week?
questions.body: Within seven days. Three specific people you will send this URL to, by name. Write the three names down now. The portfolio works when it travels.

questions.title: What is the one thing you will do this month?
questions.body: Within thirty days. One internship application, one networking conversation, or one off-campus role — where this portfolio is the proof. Write down which.

techniques: Your portfolio is a long-running asset. Update it every time you build something new.
Personal messages beat generic applications. One DM with your portfolio URL to a senior or an alum outperforms ten cold emails.
If anyone responds, even with one line, reply within twelve hours. Speed signals seriousness.
Put the portfolio URL on your LinkedIn, your resume, and your Naukri or Internshala profile. Three places, today.
Every change to your portfolio should be traceable to feedback. Not your own taste.
When you feel the urge to rebuild before sending, ask. Am I rebuilding because it is wrong, or because I am uncomfortable showing something imperfect?
Today. LinkedIn post drafted. Three people you will message identified.
Within 48 hours. Post published. Messages sent.
Next month. Apply to one internship or role where your strongest build is the proof.
Placement season. Open your portfolio in the interview. Run the 90-second demo from your phone.

build.title: Update LinkedIn now
build.instructions: Featured section: pin your Demo Day recording. About section: one sentence ending in your portfolio URL. Skills: add 'AI product development' if it is not there. Five minutes. Do it before you leave the room.

build.title: Update your resume now
build.instructions: Add one line under 'projects' or 'portfolio'. 'Built and shipped six AI products as a solopreneur in the Just Build cohort. Portfolio: yourname.justbuild.orangecaterpillar.com'. Five minutes.

build.title: Send three messages tonight
build.instructions: Three people, before you sleep. A senior. An alum. A friend who works somewhere relevant. WhatsApp or DM. One line: 'Spent six weeks building this. Thought you might want to see.' Plus the URL. Speed signals seriousness.

test_output.title: The LinkedIn test
test_output.description: Is your Demo Day recording pinned to your LinkedIn featured section before you leave this room? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The resume test
test_output.description: Is your portfolio URL on your resume, in the projects section, before you leave this room? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The three-messages test
test_output.description: Will you send the three messages before you sleep tonight? If yes, pass.
